retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Skeletal Muscle Contraction -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The cross-bridge cycle, an event that occurs during excitation-contraction coupling, refers to the mechanism by which the thick and thin filaments slide past one another to generate a muscle contraction. At the beginning of the cycle, when myosin is tightly bound to actin, no adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is bound to myosin, a state known as rigor; this is a transient state in contracting muscle, whereas, in the absence of ATP, such as in death, this state is permanent and is called rigor mortis. Next, ATP binds to the myosin head, inducing a conformational change in myosin that decreases its affinity for actin. Consequently, myosin dissociates from actin and the myosin head becomes cocked toward the end of the sarcomere. The ATP bound to myosin becomes hydrolyzed to adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and one inorganic phosphate molecule, which both remain linked to myosin. In its cocked position, myosin then binds to a new site on the actin, creating a power stroke that pulls the actin filaments. Each cross-bridge cycling event results in the myosin head progressing up the actin filament under the condition that Ca2+ remains bound to troponin C. Finally, ADP is released, and myosin returns to its original state of rigor where it is bound to actin in the absence of ATP. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscle Contraction -- Mechanism -- General Mechanism of Smooth Muscle Contraction
retrieved_docs.content: The contraction of smooth muscle is not regulated by the binding of Ca to the troponin complex, as is seen in cardiac and skeletal muscle contraction. Smooth muscle instead utilizes calmodulin, an intracellular second messenger that binds calcium. Smooth muscle contraction initiation and execution occur in the following steps. Intracellular Ca concentration increases when calcium enters the cell and is released from the SR. Calcium binds to calmodulin. Ca-calmodulin activates myosin light chain kinase (MLCK). MLCK phosphorylates myosin head light chains and increases myosin ATPase activity. Active myosin cross-bridges slide along actin and create muscle tension. Smooth muscle relaxation occurs as free Ca in the cytosol decreases when Ca is pumped out of the cell or back into the SR. Ca unbinds from calmodulin. Myosin phosphatase removes phosphate from myosin, decreasing myosin ATPase activity and muscle tension.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscle Contraction -- Cellular Level -- Striated Muscle
retrieved_docs.content: Cross-bridge cycling begins when ATP binds to an ATP-binding domain on the myosin head. Myosin dissociates from the actin, breaking the cross-bridge. ATP is then hydrolyzed into ADP and P, which causes the myosin heads to change conformation and move toward the positive end of the actin, cocking the myosin head. The phosphate is released, and the ADP-bound myosin binds to a new location on the actin filament. ADP is then released, which causes the myosin to return to its original position, pulling on the actin filament and causing the sarcomere (and, therefore, the muscle fiber) to contract. These cycles continue until calcium levels in the myocyte fall, causing tropomyosin to cover the actin filaments' myosin-binding sites. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Pathophysiology -- Muscle Contraction
retrieved_docs.content: It is crucial to grasp the fundamental physiology of muscle cell function initially. The sliding filament model represents muscle tension as a function dependent on the contraction of the muscle filaments. This contraction is facilitated by calcium released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, leading to muscle depolarization. Intracellular calcium binds to the anionic charge of troponin C, causing the displacement of tropomyosin from the G-actin site. Once exposed, a myosin head attaches to the exposed G-actin site, initiating a pivot that requires energy from adenosine triphosphate (ATP). This pivot allows actin filaments to slide past myosin filaments, resulting in muscle shortening, transmitted to the muscle cell's glycoprotein-rich cytoskeleton.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The myosin present in these cells is classified as MII. It is a hexamer molecule of 2 myosin heavy chains (MHC) and 2 pairs of myosin light chains (MLC). It forms 3 basic domains. The ‘head’ region comprises the globular N-terminal end of the MHCs that protrudes laterally from the filament. It has the actin-binding region and the ATP hydrolysis site that gives the energy needed for contraction. This is converted into a larger movement due to the stiff 'neck' domain at the C-terminal end of the motor region. [1] The 'neck' domain is also where myosin light chains bind non-covalently. The ‘tail’ domain comprises the C-terminal ends of the myosin-heavy chains, which are intertwined in an α-helical rod and form the major constituents of the thick filaments of the myocytes.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Smooth Muscle -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Steps involved in smooth muscle cell contraction: Depolarization of membrane or hormone/neurotransmitter activation L-type voltage-gated calcium channels open Calcium-induced calcium release from the SR Increased intracellular calcium Calmodulin binds calcium Myosin light chain kinase activation Phosphorylation of myosin light chain Increase myosin ATPase activity Myosin-P binds actin Cross-bridge cycling leads to muscle tone.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Contractility is impacted by a variety of intrinsic and extrinsic forces including calcium concentration. Calcium is released by the sarcoplasmic reticulum of cardiac muscle and binds to troponin C. This bond stimulates a conformational change of tropomyosin that releases it from the binding sites on the actin filament. With the release of tropomyosin, the active sites on actin are free to bind myosin which causes adenosine triphosphate (ATP) to be broken down into adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and inorganic phosphate (P). Subsequently, there is a power stroke of the myosin head which forces the actin filament centrally towards the sarcomere, and ADP and P detach from the myosin head resulting in contraction. Once ATP re-attaches to myosin, actin is freed. This cycle continues until intracellular calcium supplies are depleted, there is no longer binding between troponin C and calcium, and tropomyosin can revert to its initial confirmation to cover the binding sites on actin. Therefore, the intracellular calcium concentration is directly proportional to the contraction force [2] .

retrieved_docs.title: Tocolysis -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: From a physiologic perspective, the myometrium is responsible for the contractional effort of childbirth. Like all smooth muscle, this process is calcium-mediated. The start of a contraction does not require any nerve input nor hormonal stimulus. It begins with a spontaneous depolarization of the cell surface, which opens voltage-gated calcium channels. [3] The influx of calcium into the cells binds to intracellular calmodulin. This new complex activates myosin light chain kinase, an enzyme that phosphorylates myosin light chains, which are located on a critical portion of the myosin heads. The phosphorylation and dephosphorylation of the myosin head cause the continual bridging to actin filaments. This repetitive process results in the myometrium of contraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscle Contraction -- Cellular Level -- Striated Muscle
retrieved_docs.content: The thick filaments are made from the protein myosin, which has one pair of heavy chains and two pairs of light chains; these heavy and light chains differ from the thin and thick filaments of myofibrils. At the tail of the thick filament, the two heavy chains are intertwined in a helical formation. At the other end of the thick filament, each heavy chain is paired with two light chains, giving rise to two heads. The myosin heads have an actin-binding site that helps them attach to the thin filaments. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Muscle -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The sternocleidomastoid muscle is voluntarily controlled and thus a striated skeletal muscle. As mentioned above, the primary mechanism by which muscles operate is by overlapping of the thick and thin filaments. The proteins that predominately make up the thin and thick filaments are actin and myosin, respectively. Myosin is like a person climbing a rope where the rope is actin. Myosin continuously grabs actin, pulls it, releases, reaches, and repeats. Myosin in skeletal muscle cells activates by a rapid increase in calcium concentration released from the muscle cell’s intracellular store, the sarcoplasmic reticulum. Calcium works by binding to a protein called troponin which moves tropomyosin. Tropomyosin is the protein that wraps around actin and prevents spontaneous binding of myosin to actin; only when calcium is at high enough concentrations can myosin bind to actin. Without calcium, myosin binds to ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi). With calcium, this complex of myosin, ADP, and Pi binds to actin forming a cross-bridge. Releasing ADP and Pi causes the power stroke (the pull in the rope analogy). ATP binds myosin, which allows it to release from actin (the letting go). ATP bound to myosin is split into ADP, and Pi and in the process, myosin is cocked back to start the cycle again (the reaching). Smooth and cardiac muscle work similarly to cause shortening; however their activation is by different processes. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Skeletal Muscle -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: As long as there is adequate Ca to maintain an uncovered actin-binding site, the myosin head will form a cross-bridge with actin. The release of ADP and inorganic phosphate causes the power stroke where the myosin head moves toward the negative end of actin (toward the center of the sarcomere), displacing the actin filament and shortening the sarcomere. To complete the cycle, ADP is released, and the sarcomere returns to a state of rigor. This cycle repeats as long as Ca is bound to troponin C. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Skeletal Muscle Contraction -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The main unit of the thick filament is the large protein myosin, which is formed by two pairs of light chains, and one pair of heavy chains. The two heavy chains of myosin twist around each other to make the helical tail of the myosin, whereas the light chains interact with the heavy chains to form the two heads of the myosin at the other end. Upon the heads lies an important binding site which facilitates the interaction of myosin with actin, a protein belonging to the thin filament. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Skeletal Muscle -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Cross-bridge cycling is the mechanism by which skeletal muscle contracts. At the beginning of this cycle, myosin is bound tightly to actin in a step termed rigor. In the absence of basic physiologic energy, such as in death, this is a semi-permanent state called rigor mortis. In living tissue, this is a transient state, as ATP binding by the myosin head causes a conformational change of the myosin head that causes the release of the actin-myosin cross-link. After ATP is bound and the cross-link is released, ATP is hydrolyzed to adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and inorganic phosphate, “re-cocking” and moving the myosin head toward the positive end of actin (closer to the ends of the sarcomere). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscle Contraction -- Mechanism -- General Mechanism of Skeletal Muscle Contraction
retrieved_docs.content: The Ca ions produce attractive forces to act between actin and myosin filaments, causing them to slide alongside each other, leading to the contractile process.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscle Myocyte -- Cellular Level -- Skeletal Muscle Myocyte
retrieved_docs.content: The following are noteworthy points about muscle contractions. Skeletal muscle is under voluntary control, except for reflexes and the diaphragm during involuntary respiration. Lower motor neurons innervate these myocytes from the spinal cord and respond to the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh). When ACh binds its receptors on the myocyte, sodium (Na+) is allowed into the cell, causing the depolarization mentioned above. Maximum muscle tension occurs when there is an optimal overlap of thick and thin filaments. That is, all myosin heads can interact with actin. If the muscle fiber is stretched too far, thick and thin filament interaction decreases. Furthermore, if the muscle shortens too considerably, the large myosin heads crowd out each other, decreasing myofilament interactions. Lastly, the maximal velocity that a muscle fiber may contract will decrease as the load on the muscle increases. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.content: ATP then binds myosin and myosin disassociated with actin.

Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common locus for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether hemorrhagic, inflammatory, traumatic, dystrophic, detachment‑related, or depositional—is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate management, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1709
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق الشبكية (retinal tear) is directly linked to the need for repair of retinal tear; detachment, indicating that a retinal tear often requires surgical repair to prevent or treat detachment. This relationship highlights the importance of timely intervention for retinal tears to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 187
retrieved_docs.content: المشيمية الوراثي). Partial and complete choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect fibrotic stiffening. Focal choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and chorioretinal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) illustrate localized tissue loss and scarring that may involve both choroid and retina. The choroidea serves as the anatomic substrate for additional inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, postoperative and metastatic processes, including the entities listed above, metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, familial retinal dystrophy of the choroid, and choroidal tears. Massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي, نزيف مشيمي باثق) exemplifies the choroidea’s role as a site for hemorrhagic events. Collectively, these interconnected entities depict a comprehensive view of posterior‑segment ocular

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 266
retrieved_docs.content: and retina, leading to scarring, atrophy, detachment, neovascularization and visual impairment. Accurate identification of the specific choroidal and retinal pathology is essential for comprehensive evaluation, multidisciplinary management and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: The repair of retinal tear; detachment is described as having a broader relationship with thermal ablation (الإنفاذ الحراري), indicating that thermal ablation techniques are broadly relevant to the management and treatment of retinal tears and detachments in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1240

Collectively, these entities demonstrate how vascular, inflammatory, traumatic, surgical and systemic metabolic factors intersect to produce overlapping retinal and choroidal pathology, and recognizing each condition as a child of the H30‑H36 classification facilitates systematic diagnosis, targeted management and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1781

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 187
retrieved_docs.content: the choroid and retina also encompass atrophic, detachment, calcific, sclerotic, dystrophic and scarred entities localized to the choroidea. Age‑related choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and secondary choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية) represent degenerative thinning, while serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), retinal‑choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic retinal‑choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي) describe fluid‑ or blood‑filled separations. Choroidal calcification (تنكس حصوي) appears as focal deposits, with partial peripapillary choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), total peripapillary choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and a hereditary form (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Partial and complete choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1160
retrieved_docs.content: indicating a diffuse loss of choroidal tissue integrity. Peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis (Peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis) illustrate localized inflammatory lesions at the retinal‑choroidal interface. Choroidal detachments are also catalogued as choroidal findings. Serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي) describe separations of the choroid from the sclera due to fluid or blood accumulation. Reversal of the choroid (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) denotes a positional change of the tissue, often secondary to severe hypotony or trauma. Finally, metabolic and hereditary deposits can affect the choroid. Partial peripupillary choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect abnormal calcium accumulation within the choroidal stroma. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: complications involve serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), general choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), and choroidal tear. Language‑specific eponymous entities also include familial choroidal membrane disorder (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية) and choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي). Collectively, these interrelated entities provide a comprehensive framework for diagnosing, classifying, and managing ocular, vascular, and systemic diseases that affect the choroid, retina, and related structures, emphasizing the choroidea as the common locus for inflammatory, degenerative, hemorrhagic, depositional, traumatic, postoperative, metastatic, and scar‑forming processes.

This association highlights the importance of identifying the underlying mechanisms when evaluating patients with crushing or internal injuries, as the wording “due to” often signals a direct link between the injury and its precipitating factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic group Dis‑orders of choroid and retina (H30‑H36) comprises a wide spectrum of choroidal and retinal pathologies that share mechanisms such as vascular compromise, inflammation, neovascular growth, degeneration, trauma, postoperative change, and systemic disease influence. Structural and degenerative entities include partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, hereditary choroidal dystrophy (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), age‑related choroidal atrophy, secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), peripapillary/choroidal sclerosis (partial حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي and total حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي), and serous, ocular, or hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين, انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Traumatic and postoperative sequelae are represented by choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي), choroidal
